SomethingNew's blog

Codeforces Round #814 (Div. 1, Div. 2) Editorial
By SomethingNew, 4 years ago,

Sorry for not very good samples, and late editorial. It was our first round, so it was wary stressful for us, But we hope you liked the problems despite this!

1719A - Chip Game

Hint 1

Try to look at the parity of n,mn,m.

Hint 2

The player who wins does not depend on player's strategy.

Hint 3

The only thing that the winning player depends on is the parity of n,mn,m.

Tutorial

Note that the game will end only when the chip is in the upper right corner (otherwise you can move it 11 square to the right or up). For all moves, the chip will move n−1n−1 to the right and m−1m−1 up, which means that the total length of all moves is n+m−2n+m−2 (the length of the move is how much the chip moved per turn). Since the length of any move is odd, then after any move of Burenka, the sum of the lengths will be odd, and after any move of Tonya is even. So we can find out who made the last move in the game by looking at (n+m−2)mod2=(n+m)mod2(n+m−2)mod⁡2=(n+m)mod⁡2. With (n+m)mod2=0(n+m)mod⁡2=0, Tonya wins, otherwise Burenka.

The complexity of the solution is O(1)O(1).

1719B - Mathematical Circus

Hint 1

Instead of kk, we can consider the remainder of dividing kk by 4.

Hint 2

There is no answer for the remainder 0.

Hint 3

For all other remainders, there is an answer.

Tutorial

Note that from the number kk we only need the remainder modulo 44, so we take kk modulo and assume that 0≤k≤30≤k≤3.

If k=0k=0, then there is no answer, because the product of the numbers in each pair must be divisible by 4=224=22, that is, the product of all numbers from 11 to nn must be divisible by 2n2⋅2=2n2n2⋅2=2n, but the degree of occurrence of 22 in this sum is ⌊n2⌋+⌊n4⌋⌊n2⌋+⌊n4⌋, which is less than nn.

If k=1k=1 or k=3k=3, then we will make all pairs of the form (i,i+1)(i,i+1), where ii is odd. Then a+ka+k will be even in each pair, since aa and kk are odd, and since bb is also even, the product will be divisible by 44. Если k=2k=2, then we will do the same splitting into pairs, but in all pairs where i+1i+1 is not divisible by 44, we will swap the numbers (that is, we will make a=i+1a=i+1 and b=ib=i). Then in pairs where i+1mod4=0i+1mod⁡4=0, bb is divisible by 44 (therefore the product too), and in the rest a+ka+k is divisible by 44 (since aa and kk have a remainder 22 modulo 44).

The complexity of the solution is O(n)O(n).

1719C - Fighting Tournament

Hint 1

Who would win duels if the strongest athlete was the first in queue?

Hint 2

After what number of rounds is the strongest athlete guaranteed to be at the front of the queue?

Hint 3

Simulate the first nn rounds and write down their winners so that for each person you can quickly find out the number of wins in rounds with numbers no more than a given number.

Tutorial

Note that after the strongest athlete is at the beginning of the queue, only he will win. The strongest athlete will be at the beginning of the queue no more than after the nn-th round. Let's simulate the first nn rounds, if the jj-th athlete won in the ii-th round, then we will write down the ii number for him. Now, to answer the query (i,k)(i,k), it is enough to find the number of wins of the ii athlete in rounds with numbers j≤kj≤k, and if k>nk>n, and the strength of the ii athlete is equal to nn, then add to the answer another k−nk−n.

The complexity of the solution is O(n+qlog(n))O(n+qlog⁡(n)).

1718A2 - Burenka and Traditions (hard version)

Hint 1

Segments of length greater than 2 are not needed.

Hint for A1 1

For A1 you can use dynamic programming

Hint for A1 2

Dynamic programming where dp[i][v] means the minimum time to make aj=0aj=0 for j<ij<i and ai=vai=v. (Notice that v can be any number from 0 to 8191)

Hint 2

There is an answer in which segments of length 22 does not intersect with segments of length 11.

Hint 3

If al⊕al+1⊕…⊕ar=0al⊕al+1⊕…⊕ar=0 is executed for l,rl,r, then we can fill this segment with zeros in r−lr−l seconds using only segments of length 2.

Tutorial

There is an answer where the time spent is minimal and the lengths of all the segments taken are 1 and 2. because of the segment l,r,xl,r,x can be replaced to ⌈r−l+12⌉⌈r−l+12⌉ of segments of length 2 and 1, or rather [l,l+1,x],[l+2,l+3,x],…,[r,r,x][l,l+1,x],[l+2,l+3,x],…,[r,r,x](or [r−1,r,x][r−1,r,x] if (l−r+1)(l−r+1) even).

Note that if al⊕al+1⊕…⊕ar=0al⊕al+1⊕…⊕ar=0 is executed for l,rl,r, then we can fill the l,rl,r subsections with zeros for r−lr−l seconds with queries [l,l+1,al],[l+1,l+2,al⊕al+1],...[r−1,r,al⊕al+1⊕…⊕ar][l,l+1,al],[l+1,l+2,al⊕al+1],...[r−1,r,al⊕al+1⊕…⊕ar].

Note that if a segment of length 2 intersects with a segment of length 1, they can be changed to 2 segments of length 1.

It follows from all this that the answer consists of segments of length 1 and cover with segments of length 2. Then it is easy to see that the answer is (nn minus (the maximum number of disjoint sub-segments with a xor of 0)), because in every sub-segments with a xor of 0 we can spend 1 second less as I waited before. this amount can be calculated by dynamic programming or greedily. Our solution goes greedy with a set and if it finds two equal prefix xors(prefixl=prefixrprefixl=prefixr means that al+1⊕al+2⊕…⊕ar=0al+1⊕al+2⊕…⊕ar=0), it clears the set. 168724728

The complexity of the solution is O(nlog(n))O(nlog⁡(n)).

1718B - Fibonacci Strings

Common hint

Try to express nn(the sum of all cici) as F1+F2+…+Fm=nF1+F2+…+Fm=n.

The way of mathematicians
Hint for mathematicians 1

Let, n=F1+F2+…+Fmn=F1+F2+…+Fm, then try to find the minimum xx starting from which if there is ci=xci=x there is no answer.

Hint for mathematicians 2

xx is (the maximum sum of the Fibonacci numbers, among which there are no neighbors) + 1.

Hint for mathematicians 3

xx is F1+F3+F5+…+Fm+1F1+F3+F5+…+Fm+1 for odd mm and F2+F4+F6+…+Fm+1F2+F4+F6+…+Fm+1 for even.

Hint for mathematicians 4

xx is Fm+1+1Fm+1+1 for odd mm and Fm+1Fm+1 for even.

Hint for mathematicians 5

Try to understand if it can happen that there are two different answers in which the letter forming the block of length FmFm differs.

Hint for mathematicians 6

The correct answer is: yes if there is ci=Fmci=Fm and cj=Fmcj=Fm and this is only way.

Now, using everything you have understood, write a greedy solution.

The way of programmers
Hint for programmers 1

Try to write a greedy solution.

Hint for programmers 2

The programmer does not need a second hint, he wrote a greedy solution after the first one.

Tutorial

At the beginning, let's check that the number nn (the sum of all cici) is representable as the sum of some prefix of Fibonacci numbers, otherwise we will output the answer NO.

Let's try to type the answer greedily, going from large Fibonacci numbers to smaller ones. For the next Fibonacci number, let's take the letter with the largest number of occurrences in the string from among those that are not equal to the previous letter taken (to avoid the appearance of two adjacent blocks from the same letter, which cannot be). If there are fewer occurrences of this letter than this number, then the answer is NO. Otherwise, we will put the letter on this Fibonacci number and subtract it from the number of occurrences of this letter.If we were able to dial all the Fibonacci numbers, then the answer is YES.

Why does the greedy solution work? Suppose at this step you need to take FiFi (I will say take a number from ctct, this will mean taking FiFi letters tt from string), let's look at the maximum cxcx now, if it cannot be represented as the sum of Fibonacci numbers up to FiFi among which there are no neighbors, then the answer is no. It can be proved that if cx>Fi+1cx>Fi+1, then it is impossible to represent cxcx.

If there is exactly one number greater than or equal to FiFi at the step, then there is only one option to take a number, so the greedy solution works.

If there are two such numbers and they are equal, then the option to take a number is exactly one, up to the permutation of letters. the greedy solution works again.

If there are cj≥Fi,cx≥Fi,j≠xcj≥Fi,cx≥Fi,j≠x, then we note that the larger of the numbers cj,cxcj,cx will be greater than FiFi, if we don't take it, then at the next step this number will be greater than Fi+1Fi+1 (ii will be 1 less), according to the above proven answer will not be, so, taking the larger of the numbers is always optimal.

The complexity of the solution is O(klog(n)log(k))O(klog⁡(n)log⁡(k)).

1718C - Tonya and Burenka-179

Hint 1

The answer for k=xk=x and k=gcd(x,n)k=gcd(x,n) is the same.

Hint 2

In this problem, a general statement is useful: for any array cc of length mm, m⋅max(c1,c2,…,cm)≥c1+c2+…+cmm⋅max⁡(c1,c2,…,cm)≥c1+c2+…+cm is true.

Hint 3

Try applying the second hint for k1k1 and k2k2 divisible by k1k1, where k1,k2k1,k2 are divisors of nn.

Hint 4

We can consider only kk which equals to npnp, where pp is a simple divisor of nn.

Tutorial

Let's note that the answer for k=xk=x and k=gcd(x,n)k=gcd(x,n) is the same.

Indeed, for the number kk, we will visit numbers with indices s+ikmodns+ikmodn for ii from 00 to n−1n−1 inclusive, from this we can see that the index of the ii-th number coincides with the index of i+ngcd(k,n)i+ngcd(k,n), and if we look at two indexes, the difference between which is ll and l<ngcd(k,n)l<ngcd(k,n), then they are different, since k⋅lmodn≠0k⋅lmodn≠0, therefore, the answer is (the sum of numbers with indices s+ikmodns+ikmodn for ii from 00 to ngcd(k,n)−1ngcd(k,n)−1) ⋅gcd(k,n)⋅gcd(k,n).

Now let's prove that the first gcd(k,n)gcd(k,n) numbers are the same for (s,x)(s,x) and (s,gcd(x,n))(s,gcd(x,n)), note that only those indices that have the same remainder as ss when divided by gcd(x,n)gcd(x,n) are suitable, but there are only ngcd(k,n)ngcd(k,n) of such indices, and we proved that we should have ngcd(k,n)ngcd(k,n) of different indices, so they are all represented once, therefore the answer for k=xk=x and k=gcd(x,n)k=gcd(x,n) is the same, because the sum consists of the same numbers.

So, we need to consider only kk being divisors of nn, this is already passes tests if we write, for example, a segment tree, but we don't want to write a segment tree, so we go further, prove that for k1=xk1=x, the answer is less or equal than for k2=x⋅yk2=x⋅y if k1k1 and k2k2 are divisors of nn, why is this so?

Note that for the number kk, the answer beats for gcd(k,n)gcd(k,n) groups of numbers, so that in each group there is exactly ngcd(k,n)ngcd(k,n) and each number is in exactly one group, and for different ss the answer will be (the sum in the group that ss belongs to) ⋅gcd(k,n)⋅gcd(k,n).

Let's look at the answer for the optimal ss for k1k1, let's call the set at which it is reached tt, note that in k2k2 for different ss there are mm independent sets that are subsets of tt. Let mimi be the sum in the ii-th set. Now note that we need to prove max(m1,m2,…,my)∗y≥m1+m2+…mymax(m1,m2,…,my)∗y≥m1+m2+…my this is true for any mimi, easy to see.

So you need to iterate the divisors which equals to npnp where pp is prime, now it can be passed with a set. Hurray!

For the divisor dd, it is proposed to store answers for all pairs (s,d)(s,d), where s≤nds≤nd and the maximum among them, they can be counted initially for O(nlogn)O(nlogn) for one dd, each request is changing one of the values, this can be done for O(logn)O(logn).

The complexity of the author's solution is O(6⋅nlog(n))O(6⋅nlog⁡(n)), where 6 is the maximum number of different prime divisors of the number nn.

1718D - Permutation for Burenka

Hint 1

Try to reduce the task to a single array and tree.

Hint 2

Try to reduce the tree to a match of bipartite graph.

The first part: For each ai=0ai=0, a segment of suitable values.

The second part: The set SS and the number dd.

Hint 3

There exist LL and RR such that the answer to the query is ''YES'' if and only if L≤d≤RL≤d≤R.

Tutorial

Let's build a tree recursively starting from the segment [1,n][1,n], at each step we will choose the root of the subtree v=argmax([pl,pl+1…,pr])+l−1v=argmax⁡([pl,pl+1…,pr])+l−1, then recursively construct trees for subtrees [l,v−1],[v+1,r][l,v−1],[v+1,r] if they are not empty, then create edges from the root to the roots of these subtrees. What we got is called a Cartesian tree by array. This Cartesian tree will be mentioned further in the analysis.

It is easy to see that Cartesian trees by arrays coincide if and only if these arrays are similar. Then the necessary and sufficient condition for the array aa to be similar to pp is that for any pair u,vu,v such that uu is in the subtree vv, av>auav>au is satisfied, in other words avav is the maximum among the numbers in the subtree of the vertex vv.

Let's call the position ii initially empty if initially ai=0ai=0.

Let's call an array aa almost similar to pp if for any pair u,vu,v such that uu is in the subtree vv, av>auav>au is executed, or both positions uu, vv are initially empty.

Let's prove that if there is a way to fill in the gaps in aa to get an array almost similar to pp, then there is also a way to fill in the gaps to get a similar to pp array aa.

Indeed, let's look at the aa array almost similar to pp. let's walk through the tree recursively starting from the root. At the step with the vertex vv, we first start recursively from all the children of vv, now it is true for them that the maxima of their subtrees are in them, let's look at the maximum child vv, let it be uu, then if av>auav>au, then everything is fine, otherwise av<auav<au note that vv is initially an empty position, because for all initially non-empty positions it is true that they are maximums in their subtrees (this is easy to see in the definition), but vv is not. Note that uu is initially an empty position. Otherwise, we have never changed avav and auau, therefore, in the original array aa almost similar to pp, av>auav>au contradiction was executed, it is contradiction. so v,uv,u are initially empty, we can perform swap(av,au)swap(av,au) and everything will be executed.

After executing this algorithm, we changed only the initially empty elements and got aa similar to pp. Q.E.D.

How to check the existence of an array almost similar to pp? Let's call the number riri the minimum among all the numbers avav such that ii is in the subtree of vv. Let's call the number lili the maximum among all the numbers avav such that vv is in the subtree of ii. it is easy to see that aa is almost similar to pp if and only if for all ii, li<ai<rili<ai<ri is satisfied.

That sounds incredibly good. So, all we need is to find a matching of the set SS and number dd and segments [li,ri][li,ri] for ii that are initially empty. Now it is easy to prove that the suitable dd is a continuous segment (let's say the answer is yes for d1d1, we don't know the answer for d2d2, try looking at the alternating path from d1d1 to d2d2 in good matching for d1d1, it's easy to see that if there is such a path, then for d2d2 the answer is yes, otherwise, no. If you look at the structure of the matching of points with segments, you can see that an alternating path exists for a continuous segment of values d2d2). the boundaries can be found by binary search or by typing greedily twice.

Final complexity is O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n) or O(nlog2n)O(nlog2⁡n)

1718E - Impressionism

Hint 1

you can notice that a pair of operations 1ij1ij, 2kt2kt and 2kt2kt, 1ij1ij lead to the same result, what does this mean?

Hint 2

This means that operations can be represented as two permutations describing which swaps will occur with rows and which swaps will occur with columns.

Hint 3

Try to reduce this problem to a graph isomorphism problem.

Tutorial

Let's have two permutations: pp of length nn and qq of length mm. Initially, both permutations are identical. For operation 1ij1ij, we will execute swap(pi,pj)swap⁡(pi,pj), for operation 2ij2ij we will execute swap(qi,qj)swap⁡(qi,qj). It is easy to see that after performing all operations in the position i,ji,j will be api,qjapi,qj.

Now we need to find permutations of p,qp,q so that api,qj=bi,japi,qj=bi,j.

Let's look at a bipartite graph where the first part is rows and the second is columns. Let if ai,j≠0ai,j≠0, then from ii there is an undirected edge in jj of the color ai,jai,j. if we construct the same graph for bb, then we need to check for isomorphism two bipartite graphs, each edge of which has a color, and as we remember, by the condition of the vertex, 2 edges of the same color cannot be Incidence. Let's call them graph aa and graph bb.

This problem is largely for implementation, therefore, starting from this moment, the actions that you can observe in the author's solution are described 168728958.

Let n<mn<m otherwise let's swap nn and mm in places. Let's try to match the elements of the array aa to the elements of the array bb. Let's go through the indices from 11 to nn. Let's now choose which pair of bb to choose for the ii-th vertex of the first fraction of the graph aa. If we have already found a pair earlier, skip the step, otherwise let's sort out the appropriate option for the pair among all the vertices of the first component bb that do not yet have a pair, there are no more than nn, after the vertices are matched, the edges are also uniquely matched (because there are no 2 edges of different colors), let's then run the substitution recursively for edges (if we have mapped the vertex vv to the vertex uu and from vv there is an edge of color xx in ii, and from uu there is an edge xx in jj, then it is easy to prove that in the answer ii will be mapped to jj), which means we will restore the entire component. Let the sum of the number of vertices and the number of edges in the component be kk, then we will perform no more than n⋅kn⋅k actions to restore the component.

by permutations, getting a sequence of swaps is a matter of technique, I will not explain it, but this is a separate function in the author's solution you will understand.

in total, our solution works for O(n(n⋅m+n+m))O(n(n⋅m+n+m)) or O(min(n,m)(n⋅m+n+m))O(min(n,m)(n⋅m+n+m)), where n⋅m+n+mn⋅m+n+m is the total number of vertices and edges in all components.

feel free to ask any questions

1718F - Burenka, an Array and Queries

Hint

The only one hint to this problem is don't try to solve, it's not worth it.

Tutorial

In order to find the number of numbers from 11 to CC that are mutually prime with xx, we write out its prime divisors (various). Let these be prime numbers a1,a2,…,aka1,a2,…,ak. Then you can find the answer for 2k2k using the inclusion-exclusion formula.

Because ⌊ab⋅c⌋=⌊⌊ab⌋c⌋⌊ab⋅c⌋=⌊⌊ab⌋c⌋, a similar statement is made for kk numbers (you can divide completely in any order).

Let's split the primes up to 2×1042×104 into 2 classes — small (≤42≤42) and large (the rest). There are 13 small numbers, and 2249 large ones. Separately, we will write down pairs of large numbers that in the product give a number not exceeding 105105. There will be 4904 such pairs.

Let's match each set of small primes with a mask. Let's write dp[mask]dp[mask] — alternating sum over the maskmask submasks of numbers ⌊Ca1⋅a2⋅…⋅ak⌋⌊Ca1⋅a2⋅…⋅ak⌋, where a1,…,aka1,…,ak — prime numbers from the submask. Similarly, we define dp[mask][big]dp[mask][big] (in addition to the mask, there is a large prime bigbig) and dp[mask][case]dp[mask][case] (in addition to the mask, there are a pair of primes from a pair of large primes casecase). Each dpdp can be counted for states×bitsstates×bits, where statesstates — is the number of states in dpdp, and bitsbits — is the mask size (number of bits).

If we write out all the large primes on the segment for which maskmask — mask of small primes, the answer for this segment will be the sum of dp[mask]+∑dp[mask][bigi]+∑dp[mask][casei]dp[mask]+∑dp[mask][bigi]+∑dp[mask][casei] (for bigbig and casecase lying on the segment). Thus, the request can be answered in 7000 calls to dpdp.

In order to find a set of prime numbers on a segment, you can use the MO algorithm.

Final complexity is O(nn−−√+q×(π(m)+casesCount(C)))O(nn+q×(π(m)+casesCount(C)))

It is worth noting that (with a very strong desire) it is possible to further optimize the solution using avx, to speed up the calculation of the amount by submasks in dynamics by 16 times, to speed up the calculation of the amount of dp[mask][big]dp[mask][big] by 8 times, which will allow you to answer the request in ~5000 (instead of 7000) calls to dpdp, and the pre-calculation for 4⋅1074⋅107 instead of 7⋅1087⋅108 (in fact, the pre-calculation works much faster due to compiler optimizations).

Please rate the problems, it will help us make the problems better next time!

    4 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    +66

    I think it's by far the hardest part of the entire problem, so the editorial's claim that it's easy is quite baffling.

    But here's how someone explained it to me: by Hall's theorem, for there to be a matching between xx and intervals, you want for all sets of intervals II, the number of elements of SS inside these intervals must be greater than the number of intervals (let's say x(I)≥N(I)x(I)≥N(I)). In other words, your added value dd is valid if and only if it is inside the union of intervals for every set II such that x(I)<N(I)x(I)<N(I).

    Now the trick is to realize that sets of intervals whose union is not a contiguous segment don't matter -- if a set whose union is two or more disjoint parts has x(I)<N(I)x(I)<N(I), then definitely one of the parts violates the condition as well, and that part alone would be a more restrictive condition on the added value dd. Therefore the valid values of dd are in the intersection of some contiguous segments, which is a contiguous segment.

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      God_Of_Blunder
      4 years ago, show (+3) #
      »
      »
      »
      God_Of_Blunder
      4 years ago, hide # ^ |
      ← Rev. 2 →  
      +28

      Wow!! Really nice proof. My approach didn't utilize this observation, it might be interesting to you.

      Approach: The problem is same that we have kk intervals and k−1k−1 points, one query point and we have to check whether we can perfectly match them or not.

      The idea is to find those intervals, removing which we can perfectly match the given k−1k−1 points and remaining k−1k−1 intervals.

      Let's match the intervals greedily:

      • Sort the intervals according to left endpoints

      • Sort the points in increasing order

      • Move from left to right and for the current point say xx insert all right endpoint values in a set whose left endpoint ≤x≤x(can be done using a pointer).

      • Match the point with the segment having the smallest rr value.

      • Also for each point, we can calculate what it will be matched to if the segment in the previous step was removed (-1 for those points that don't have such an option).

      In the end, the set should have exactly one spare segment.

      Let segisegi be the segment alloted to ithith (1≤i≤k−1)(1≤i≤k−1) interval and segksegk be the only spare segment.

      We can be sure that we can match segksegk to dd ensuring that the remaining segments still have a perfect match.

      Let's make a DP where dpidpi denotes whether we can remove segisegi such that the remaining segments perfectly match with given k−1k−1 points. Initially, segk=truesegk=true.

      • Iterate from k−1k−1 to 11, and for each segisegi we know the segment it will be replaced with (i.e the segment that will be matched to intervaliintervali) if segisegi didn't exist, let it be nxtinxti.
      • nxtinxti is guaranteed to be greater than ii because that segment would have been allotted later only to some point.
      • dpi=dpnxtidpi=dpnxti

      Finally, we have all the segments in which dd can lie. Assuming segments do not always form a contiguous section, we can answer queries offline (using sweepline) or online (using segment tree).

      Code

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        maxplus
        4 years ago, show #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        maxplus
        4 years ago, hide # ^ |
         
        +13

        "Transposed" version of your solution can be used to prove that "suitable dd will form a contiguous segment" as well (and as a bonus, with a few standard tricks it can be implemented in O(nα(n))O(nα(n)), faster not only in theory but in practice).

        • Store SS in a set, sort segments in the order of increasing right ends.
        • Iterate through segments.
        • Greedily assign to the current segment the first point to the right of its left end, removing it from the set.
        • If such a point can't be assigned (either it's beyond the right end of the segment or there's no such point), we know that another point must be added somewhere to the left of the right end of this segment, so we get an upper bound RR on dd and we pretend to match this segment with RR.
        • If such situation happens a second time, even "optimal" dd wasn't enough to save SS, so for every dd the answer is no.
        • Repeat the same from another direction, sorting segments in the order of decreasing left ends, finding a lower bound LL. We don't have to continue after finding the first problematic segment, since if LL being in its own greedy way optimal isn't good enough, RR couldn't have been good enough either.
        • If we determined that there is at least some solution for dd, imagine adding d=Ld=L and again matching segments with points from the left, as we did looking for RR. Without this additional point there was a segment that had no matching point, but with LL it must have a match -- either it matched LL itself, or some previous segment did and every segment after shifted its match one point left, and some point eventually "carried over" to the problematic segment.
        • If we shift the added point from LL to some position x,L<x<Rx,L<x<R, up until this point segments will be matched to the same points as without xx. The segment that will try to match xx was able to match both a point to the left (when we added LL, which might be this "point to the left" of xx too) and a point to the right (when we added no point) (except if it is the problematic segment, then there is no suitable point to the right, but x<Rx<R so xx satisfies the right end of this segment), so it can match xx, and every next segment will match the same point as with LL added. So every such xx is good too.
        Tricks for better complexity
        • Cartesian tree construction in O(n)O(n).
        • Emulating set without insertions as "cross-out list" based on DSU.
        • Radix sort with big base ._.
        → Reply

  • »
    »
    Andalus
    4 years ago, show (+6) #
    »
    »
    Andalus
    4 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    +1

    Yeah, the editorial should definitely elaborate on that. And some of these observations aren't even needed for D1.

    My DP approach: the optimal solution for an array of nn elements can be constructed by taking the optimal solution for the first kk elements (for some k<nk<n) and then applying the XOR chain (overlapping segments of size 2) on the rest of the elements. We need to try each value of kk, however, which leads to an O(n2)O(n2) solution, maintaining both an array of optimal time as well as an array of accumulating XOR chain results for each possible starting index. This doesn't work in D1.

    Greedy approach: maintain a running XOR while also storing each intermediate result in a set. If a result appears twice, i.e., r⊕ai⊕⊕ai+1⊕⋯⊕ai+k=rr⊕ai⊕⊕ai+1⊕⋯⊕ai+k=r, then the subarray ai,…,ai+k]ai,…,ai+k] has a XOR sum of 0. We don't need to know where the subarray starts (ii), but we just need to detect when it ends (when the running XOR is not a new value), so we can update the saving counter and reset the running XOR chain. You can check out just how simple it is over here: https://codeforces.com/contest/1719/submission/168640804

    That being said, the observation that a running XOR will encounter an old element if a subarray has a XOR sum of 0 should definitely be included in the editorial, in my opinion, since that's essential to the greedy approach (unless the authors had a different greedy approach in mind).

    → Reply

      ks89417
      3 years ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      why we dont need to know where the subarray starts??

      Say I have XOR equal to 0 for a subarray from index 4....20 and inside that we have a subarray from index 9....16 which have its XOR = 0 as well Then shouldn't not considering where the subarray would start be wrong in this case as I would have 2 saves in this case while if I clear the set when reaching index 16 then how would I know about the save for 4....20 ??

      Just curious about this and couldn't actually understand how this works.

      Any help would be really appreciated :)

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        Andalus
        3 years ago, show #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        Andalus
        3 years ago, hide # ^ |
         
        0

        We only want to count the number of non-overlapping subarrays with XOR of 0. If a 0-XOR subarray is inside a larger 0-XOR subarray, we will not benefit from exploiting the larger subarray, since we don't want to waste time in revisiting the smaller subarray.

        For your example, if we find that 9-16 has a XOR of 0, then we can save 1 second from the 9-16 subarray. The "saving" means that we can perform the operation on [9, 10], [10, 11], [11, 12], [13, 14], [14, 15], and [15, 16]. We always choose xx as the current value of the first index we pick (so the first index turns into 0). Since indices 9-16 have a XOR value of 0, the latest operation of [15, 16] will also turn index 16 to 0, allowing us to make the 9-16 subarray (7 elements) into all 0s in 6 seconds.

        The fact that the subarray from 4-20 also has a XOR of 0 is not something we can exploit anymore. It's true that, if we then perform the operation on pairs like [4, 5], [5, 6], ..., [18, 19], and [19, 20], then we will get them all to 0, but this requires revisiting indices 9 to 16, which are already 0! The operations from [8, 9] to [16, 17] take 8 seconds to perform, when it would have been much faster to just apply the operation on [8] alone, and then move on to [17, 18] and so on.

        So it makes no sense to try to take advantage of BOTH the 9-16 subarray and the 4-20 subarray, because the moment you decide to exploit the 9-16 subarray, you turn this range into 0 (saving 1 second) and you don't want to touch this 9-16 subarray ever again. Now, it's possible that we could instead decide to exploit the 4-20 subarray directly without exploiting the 9-16 array; however, it's always better to exploit the subarray that ends the earliest first, since then you have more indices available for future exploits (e.g., if there is a subarray from say, 18-25 that has a XOR of 0, then we can save 1 second from 9-16 and another 1 second from 18-25; whereas if we instead decided to save 1 second from 4-20, then we are no longer able to save another second through 18-25 since 18-20 have been zero'd out).

        Let me know if you're still confused about anything here.

        → Reply

      Well, the above code is fairly simple, but actually I still do not really understand why this works.

      What is the key idea we can see here instantly?

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »

        Yahia_Emara
        4 years ago, show #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        Yahia_Emara
        4 years ago, hide # ^ |
        ← Rev. 2 →  
        0

        The Key Idea is to notice that the extra XOR that comes to a[i] before turning a[i] to zero with one operation can be represented as the xor subarray ending at index i-1 (can be empty) (where this subarray represents consecutive subarray of size 2 before going to index i), so to make a[i] already 0 before reaching it, we have to find a subarray ending at index i with xor sum equal to 0 (i.e find maximum j such that pref[i]^pref[j] = 0)

        → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        MrOtter
        4 years ago, show #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        MrOtter
        4 years ago, hide # ^ |
        ← Rev. 3 →  
        +8

        As far as I understood:
        You can always use n steps to turn all elements to zero by just taking all indexes consecutively. And you want to improve this result. How you can do it?
        You notice that there's no sense to use range of size greater than 2 since the given formula of seconds calculation. What can you do with this?
        If you take two numbers a[i] and a[i+1] and apply xor with a[i] to them you can get two results:
        1) [0,0] it means a[i]==a[i+1] => you saved 1 second here!
        2) [0,X] now you can apply xor with X to next pair [X,a[i+2]] and so on. you can apply it until you get [0,0] pair or get to end of array and turn last element to zero with xor with range of 1. However you saved 1 second!
        So what do these both situations have in common? xor of all elements in covered ranges equal to 0.
        so now you can convert problem into finding count of subarrays with xor=0 (every subarray is a saved 1 second) and the answer will be n-(count of such subarrays)

        → Reply

Another approach for Div2 C, based on Editorial one.

Let's calculate next greater element and previous greater element. Then, for each aiai, we can calculate how many rounds athlete will win for infinite kk. If previous greater element exist, he won't win a single round. Otherwise, the answer equals to distance between athlete and its next greater element.

When we answering queries, one should notice that an athlete start to fight after i−1i−1 rounds — this is the time he should wait to get into second index and start fighting.

Time complexity: O(n)O(n)

https://codeforces.com/contest/1719/submission/168605787

  • »

    RDFZzzx
    4 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    RDFZzzx
    4 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 3 →  
    +1

    There are 22 conditions in this problem.

    I use tmptmp to mark the number of the steps to make the biggest value to the front of the array.

    And array cnticnti is used to count the number of the victories of the i_th player.

    If k<tmpk<tmp

    There are two subtask in this conditions:

    • validvalid is the biggest number (valid==nvalid==n) then the answer should be cnt[id] + k - tmp, because this player will win after round k−tmp−1k−tmp−1

    • Otherwise : the answer is cnticnti.

    Otherwise

    There are also two conditions:

    • If validvalid is the biggest number in the prefix.

    I use array rr to mark the first element which is on its right and has a bigger value than it ( you can use a stack to get array rr in O(n)O(n) in the begining.

    The answer of this condition is (id != 1) + min(r[id] - id - 1, k - id + 1)

    • Otherwise : the answer is 00

    In this way, I can get each answer in O(1)O(1) and use O(n)O(n) time to get tmptmp and array cntcnt and rr.

    → Reply

  • »

    Wayne_Yan
    4 years ago, show #

    Wayne_Yan
    4 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    assume the answer is YES, with n+1 blocks total, then we can prove that the number of letters with third highest frequency cant be greater than F_n. If the last block used the letter with second highest frequency, then we can prove that ( because F_n = F_(n-1) + F_(n-2) ) the next two segments has to BOTH be from the letter from maximum frequency, that leads to a contradiction. Therefore the last block has to come from the letter of max frequency.

    the parts I didn't prove can be done by messy but routine bounding

+36

Another way to understand the solution to div1B is the Zeckendorf Theorem, which states that each positive integer can be uniquely represented by the sum of a set of unique fibonacci numbers where no two are adjacent.

We can apply this representation directly to the frequency of each letter and solve the problem.

And due to this uniqueness of the representation, the greedy solution will also always work.

→ Reply

    4 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +1

    Nope, not a bug. Looks like you failed system tests.

    So, to put it short, the test cases that are run on your code during the contest are called pretests. These are normally not too big but are certainly meant to be strong. after the round is over, there will be system tests, which are basically extra cases added on to the pretests to make sure that your solution is indeed efficient/correct

I have a solution with bitset in O(nClognp(k)+nClognw+qCw+2kCw)O(nClog⁡np(k)+nClog⁡nw+qCw+2kCw).

We just calculate the bitset of every subset of the first kk primes. For larger primes, we can use the trick called "猫树分治"(i don't know how to translate it into English, maybe it's called "cat tree divide and conquer").

Since std::bitset have an extremely small constant, we can solve this problem by this weird method.

Edit:

p(k)p(k) is the kk-th prime, i can't prove its exact bound, but it has a small constant.

Edit2:

I think k=14k=14 or k=15k=15 would be the fastest one, but it takes too much memory. It runs 1668 ms with k=13k=13.

→ Reply

    0

    if prefix_xor[i] = prefix_xor[j] then a[i+1] ^ a[i + 2] ^ ... ^ a[j] = 0, disjoint sub-segments means disjoint subarrays.

I don't think the editorial of 1E is understandable, but my poor English doesn't allow me to write a whole editorial.

But I can give a proof for the time complexity. Consider divide the connected components of the two graphs into groups, such that each component in a group has the same number of left vertices. Then we can consider only matchings between vertices that their connected components are in the same group.

Now we only consider components in the ii-th group. Let the xixi be the number of left vertices in each component, yiyi be the number of components in the graph aa, zizi be the number of edges in the graph bb. If xi=0xi=0, each of the components is made up of only a right vertices. It's easy to deal with this situation.

Otherwise xi≠0xi≠0. Consider a component in aa and a vertex uu in it, we are looking for a vertex to match with uu. For an edge in bb, suppose the edge belongs to the component SS, then the edge will contribute to the time only when we try to match uu with some left vertex in SS. So the time complexity of dealing with this group is O(xiyizi)O(xiyizi), and the total time is ∑xiyizi≤(∑xiyi)(∑zi)=O(n2m)∑xiyizi≤(∑xiyi)(∑zi)=O(n2m). Now we can solve the whole problem in O(nmnm−−−√)O(nmnm) time.

→ Reply

Can anyone find out my error in this code? It WA on test 2.560 :(

#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

int a[100005];
vector<int> ans[100005];
deque<int> que;

int main() {
	int t;
	scanf("%d", &t);
	while (t--) {
		int n, q;
		scanf("%d %d", &n, &q);
		int before;
		for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
			ans[i].clear();
			scanf("%d", &a[i]);
			que.push_back(a[i]);
			if (a[i] == n) {
				before = i;
			}
		}
		for (int i = 0; i < before; i++) {
			int one = que.front();
			que.pop_front();
			int two = que.front();
			que.pop_front();
			ans[max(one, two)].push_back(i);
			que.push_front(max(one, two));
			que.push_back(min(one, two));
		}
		for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
			que.pop_back();
			cerr << ans[a[i]].size() << " ";
		}
		cerr << endl;
		while (q--) {
			int i, k;
			scanf("%d %d", &i, &k);
			i--;
			if (k <= before) {
				printf("%d\n", upper_bound(ans[a[i]].begin(), ans[a[i]].end(), k - 1) - ans[a[i]].begin());
			} else {
				if (a[i] == n) {
					printf("%d\n", k - before + (i != 0));
				} else {
					printf("%d\n", ans[a[i]].size());
				}
			}
		}
	}
	return 0;
}

"Note that if a segment of length 2 intersects with a segment of length 1, they can be changed to 2 segments of length 1."

I just did the basic 2-length segment thing, which fails 183824117. I cannot find a case where it fails though. Please if somebody could help me out. Thanks!

Another stupid solution to div1F:

  • First, do divide and conquer on the queries to remap them to "answer this query given the set of these prime numbers in my range" (storing them in a bitset of O(primes under M) size)

  • Then, sort the queries in bitset lexicographical order (where primes[0] = 2, primes[1] = 3, etc.). Process them left to right, and process adjacent differences in bitsets; whenever we add/remove a bit, naively do O(C / prime[i]) work on all of the multiples of the number to maintain how many times that multiple is hit. We want to maintain how many indices are non-zero.

To really squeeze and cache optimize this, make everything chars since max # distinct prime factors under 2e4 = 6.

you can show that the sum of O(C / prime[i]) ops takes ~2e4 * MAXC operations in the worst case [sum of 2 *(1/2) + 4 * (1/3) + 8 * (1/5)... until we exhaust (max # distinct prime factors) * N terms] * 2 * MAXC, but I guess 2e9 simple operations are fast enough.

→ Reply
